questions.text: Between 1700 and 1995, what percentage of the world's total area was cleared for industrial uses, cultivation, etc.?
questions.explanation: During the period of industrialisation, 9.3% of the world’s total area was cleared.

questions.text: Why did the British need forests for the Royal Navy?
questions.explanation: By the 1820s, oak forests in England were disappearing, so they turned to India for a steady supply of timber for ships.

questions.text: What created a new demand for timber in India from the 1850s?
questions.explanation: Timber was essential to build railway tracks and sleepers to hold the tracks together.

questions.text: How many sleepers were required for each mile of railway track?
questions.explanation: Each mile of track required between 1,760 and 2,000 sleepers.

questions.text: Who was the first Inspector General of Forests in India?
questions.explanation: Dietrich Brandis, a German expert, was appointed as the first Inspector General of Forests in India.

questions.text: The Imperial Forest Research Institute was set up in 1906 at:
questions.explanation: The Forest Research Institute was established in Dehradun to teach scientific forestry.

questions.text: In 'Scientific Forestry', natural forests were replaced by:
options: Plantations (one type of tree in straight rows)
questions.explanation: Natural forests with many types of trees were cut down and replaced with one type of tree planted in rows.

questions.text: The Forest Act of 1878 divided forests in India into ______ categories.
questions.explanation: The Act divided forests into Reserved, Protected, and Village forests.

questions.text: Which category of forest was considered the 'best' and villagers could not take anything from it?
questions.explanation: Reserved forests were the best forests; villagers were forbidden from using them.

questions.text: What was 'shifter cultivation' or 'swidden agriculture' called in India?
questions.explanation: Jhum or Dhya is the local name for shifting cultivation in India.

questions.text: Why did Europeans ban shifting cultivation?
options: It made it harder to calculate taxes
Danger of flames spreading and burning timber
Land could not grow trees for railway timber
questions.explanation: European foresters regarded shifting cultivation as harmful to 'scientific' forest management.

questions.text: Which community in Bastar is NOT mentioned as a local tribe?
questions.explanation: Bhotiyas are a pastoral community of the Himalayas, not Bastar.

questions.text: The rebellion in the kingdom of Bastar in 1910 was led by:
questions.explanation: Gunda Dhur, from the village of Nethanar, was an important figure in the Bastar rebellion.

questions.text: The people of Bastar rebelled because the British government proposed to:
options: Stop hunting and collection of forest produce
questions.explanation: A combination of these restrictive policies led the tribes to revolt in 1910.

questions.text: What symbols were circulated to invite people to the Bastar rebellion?
questions.explanation: Mango boughs, a lump of earth, chillies and arrows were used as messages to rally the villages.

questions.text: Java was a colony of which European power?
questions.explanation: The Dutch started forest management in Java, which was then their colony.

questions.text: Which community in Java were skilled forest cutters and shifting cultivators?
questions.explanation: The Kalangs of Java were a community of skilled forest cutters; they even revolted against the Dutch in 1770.

questions.text: Who challenged the Dutch state's ownership of the forest in Java around 1890?
questions.explanation: Surontiko Samin of Randublatung village began questioning state ownership of the forest.

questions.text: What was the 'Blandongdiensten' system in Java?
options: Exemption from taxes in exchange for providing free labor for forest work
questions.explanation: The Dutch first imposed rents on land and then exempted some villages if they worked for free to cut and transport timber.

questions.text: The 'Scorched Earth' policy was followed by the ______ in Java during WWII.
questions.explanation: The Dutch destroyed sawmills and burnt logs of teak so they would not fall into Japanese hands.

questions.text: A 'sleeper' in railways refers to:
options: Horizontal planks of wood on which railway lines are laid
questions.explanation: Sleepers hold the tracks in place and were traditionally made of hard wood.

questions.text: Which of these trees was considered most suitable for building ships and railways by the British?
questions.explanation: Hardwoods like Teak and Sal were preferred for their strength and durability.

questions.text: What happened to the 'Kalangs' when they refused to work for the Dutch?
options: They revolted and attacked a Dutch fort
questions.explanation: The Kalangs rose in rebellion in 1770 against the Dutch at Joana.

questions.text: According to the 1878 Act, which forest was used by villagers for their own use?
questions.explanation: Villagers could take wood from village forests for house building or fuel.

questions.text: Bastar is located in the southernmost part of which state?
questions.explanation: Bastar is a district in the state of Chhattisgarh.

questions.text: Who said 'the state had not created the wind, water, earth and wood, so it could not own it'?
questions.explanation: This was the core argument of the Saminist movement in Java.

questions.text: The British encouraged the production of commercial crops like jute, sugar, and wheat because:
options: They were needed for the growing urban population and industrial production in Europe
questions.explanation: The demand for food and raw materials for industry drove the expansion of cultivation.

questions.text: Between 1880 and 1920, cultivated area in India rose by ______ million hectares.
questions.explanation: The colonial period saw a massive expansion in agricultural land at the cost of forests.

questions.text: In shifting cultivation, the ash from burnt trees provides:
questions.explanation: The seeds are sown in the ashes after the first monsoon rains.

questions.text: A 'taungya' is a system where:
options: Local farmers were allowed to cultivate temporary crops within a forest plantation
questions.explanation: It was a system used in Burma and India to manage forest plantations with local help.

questions.text: Forest Acts changed the lives of villagers by making ______ an illegal activity.
questions.explanation: Common daily activities became 'crimes' and people were forced to steal from forests.

questions.text: Which of these products do we NOT get from forests?
questions.explanation: Forests provide various products like gum, oil, and food, but petroleum is a fossil fuel found underground.

questions.text: In the Bastar rebellion, why did people attack the houses of officials and traders?
options: Because they represented colonial exploitation and high prices
They were encouraged by the Dutch
questions.explanation: The rebellion targeted those seen as part of the oppressive colonial system.

questions.text: The British managed to suppress the Bastar rebellion but it took them ______ months.
questions.explanation: It took from February to May 1910 to regain control of the region.

questions.text: One-third of Java's population fled the Saminist movement when the Dutch came to measure their land.
questions.explanation: Saminists protested by lying on their land when the Dutch came to survey it, or by refusing to pay taxes.

questions.text: Modern forest management today focuses on ______ instead of just timber.
questions.explanation: Governments now recognize that local people are essential for protecting forests.

questions.text: The 1865 Forest Act was amended in ______ and 1927.
questions.explanation: The 1878 amendment was a major turning point in restrictive forest laws.

questions.text: Mahua is used for:
questions.explanation: Mahua flowers are edible and used to make alcohol; the seeds are used for oil.

questions.text: In Southeast Asia, shifting cultivation is called:
questions.explanation: Lading is the name used in parts of Southeast Asia.

questions.text: The Bastar rebellion was finally 'defeated' when Gunda Dhur was captured.
questions.explanation: The British could never capture Gunda Dhur despite their victory.
